title: Bipolar Affective Disorder
oneLiner: Cyclical mood disorder with episodes of mania/hypomania and depression; high suicide risk; requires long-term mood stabilisation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Delirium (organic cause e.g. infection, metabolic, stroke):** Marked by fluctuating consciousness, disorientation, visual hallucinations — never assume “mania” without ruling out organic brain syndrome.
- **Lithium toxicity (if on treatment):** Ataxia, coarse tremor, confusion, vomiting; urgent level needed — delays can be fatal.
sections.Presentation:
- **Mania:** Elevated/irritable mood, pressured speech, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, reckless behaviour (spending, sexual disinhibition), psychosis (delusions congruent with mood).
- **Hypomania:** Same but less severe, no psychosis, not causing major functional impairment.
- **Depression:** Low mood, anhedonia, psychomotor retardation/agitation, poor concentration, suicidal ideation.
- **Mixed episode:** Both manic and depressive symptoms simultaneously — highest suicide risk.
- **Rapid cycling:** ≥4 episodes per year.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Organic mania (hyperthyroidism, Cushing’s, medication-induced):** Check TFT, urine toxicology, cortisol if atypical features.
- **Unipolar mania (rare):** No major depressive episodes — often indistinguishable initially; history of depressive episodes is key.
- **Borderline personality disorder:** Affective instability with emptiness, identity disturbance, chronic suicidality — lack of clear episodes and absence of decreased need for sleep helps differentiate.
- **ADHD (in younger adults):** Childhood-onset, sustained inattention/hyperactivity, no discrete episodes; mania is episodic with cyclic mood.
- **Substance intoxication (cocaine, amphetamines, steroids):** Urine toxicology positive; onset linked to use.
- **Schizoaffective disorder:** Psychotic symptoms occur outside mood episodes (≥2 weeks without prominent mood symptoms).
- **Splitter:** Decreased need for sleep (not just inability to sleep) is highly specific for mania vs. affective instability in BPD.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** ECG (QTc before antipsychotics); vital signs (including temperature for NMS); urine dip for pregnancy (all women of childbearing age); urine toxicology.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT (thyrotoxicosis mimics mania; lithium affects thyroid), B12/folate (if cognitive deficit), syphilis/HIV serology (if atypical), pregnancy test (if female). **Lithium level** (if known treatment) — toxic or subtherapeutic.
- **Imaging:** CT head (if first episode after 40, focal neurology, or atypical features); CT/MRI if confusion persists.
- **Special tests:** Neuropsychological testing if cognitive decline.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** DSM-5 — **Mania:** elevated/irritable mood + ≥3 of: grandiosity, decreased sleep, pressured speech, flight of ideas, distractibility, increased goal-directed activity, risky behaviour; lasting ≥1 week, causing functional impairment. **Hypomania:** same but ≥4 days, no significant impairment, no psychosis.
- **Never-miss test:** **Thyroid function** — hyperthyroidism can cause mania; untreated can worsen lithium safety. Also: **pregnancy test** before starting valproate or lithium (teratogenic).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess agitation/risk: consider benzodiazepine (lorazepam 1–2 mg IM/PO) +/- antipsychotic (haloperidol 5–10 mg IM, olanzapine 10 mg IM) — monitor QTc.
  - Safety: if imminent risk to self/others, consider urgent referral under **Mental Health Act** for detention.
  - If lithium toxicity suspected: stop lithium, get urgent level, consider IV fluids, discuss with renal/poisons.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Start **mood stabiliser** (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine for depressive phase) under specialist guidance.
  - For severe mania: lithium plus antipsychotic (e.g. olanzapine 10–20 mg od) or valproate.
  - For bipolar depression: lamotrigine or quetiapine (start low, up-titrate).
  - Monitor side effects: lithium levels (weekly until stable), renal function, thyroid, weight, metabolic profile.
  - Consider **1:1 observations** if high suicide/self-harm risk.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Maintenance: continue mood stabiliser (lithium first-line for prevention) — target level 0.6–1.0 mmol/L.
  - Psychoeducation, lifestyle (sleep hygiene, avoiding stimulants), relapse prevention plan.
  - Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) if refractory or mild symptoms.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1L 0.9% sodium chloride over 8 hours if dehydrated (common in mania), then as oral.
  - VTE prophylaxis: if prolonged immobility (e.g. catatonia) — enoxaparin 40 mg SC od.
  - Diet: normally as tolerated, high-calorie if underweight in mania.
  - Observations: if risk >5 on NEWS2, hourly; otherwise 4-hourly; dedicated mental health checks at least every shift.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Lithium (carbonate/acetate):** Start [check local policy – typical 300–400 mg od/bd], titrate to serum level 0.6–1.0 mmol/L (acute mania up to 1.2). Duration: indefinite for maintenance.
  - **Valproate (sodium valproate):** Start [check local policy – 500–1000 mg daily in divided doses], adjust to plasma level 50–100 mg/L. Duration: acute and maintenance.
  - **Lamotrigine (for depression):** Start 25 mg od, slow up-titration (weekly increase) to 200 mg od; risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
  - **Olanzapine (acute mania):** 10–20 mg PO/IM od (max 20 mg). QTc monitoring required.
  - **Quetiapine (mania or depression):** 300–600 mg/day (for mania); 150–300 mg/day for depression.
- **Common alternatives:** Carbamazepine (second-line), haloperidol (agitation), aripiprazole.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Lithium: avoid with NSAIDs, ACEi, thiazides; contraindicated in severe renal impairment.
  - Valproate: contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic), hepatic disease.
  - Lamotrigine: interaction with valproate (increases level, slower titration needed).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Lithium (renally cleared): reduce dose if eGFR <30; check levels frequently.
  - Valproate (hepatic metabolism): caution in liver disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Lithium — starting at full dose without checking baseline renal/thyroid or monitoring levels** → risk of toxicity/neurological harm. **Lamotrigine — rapid dose escalation causing Stevens-Johnson syndrome** (must follow titration protocol).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Lithium toxicity, neuroleptic malignant syndrome (antipsychotics), suicide attempt, violent behaviour.
- **Chronic:** Cognitive decline (especially with chronic lithium or repeated episodes), metabolic syndrome (weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidaemia from antipsychotics), osteoporosis (with long-term lithium), renal impairment (lithium – tubular damage), non-adherence.
sections.Prognosis: - Life-long illness; ~50% relapse within 1 year if medication stopped. With optimal maintenance, episodes can be reduced by 60–70%. Suicide risk ~15% lifetime (especially in mixed/rapid cycling). Functional outcome variable; early treatment and good adherence improve outcome.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check thyroid and renal function before starting lithium** — cannot monitor without baseline.
- **Document capacity to consent every visit** — mania can impair decision-making; if declining treatment, consider MHA.
- **Check QT interval on ECG before giving antipsychotics** — especially if combined with other QT-prolonging drugs.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Prescribing antidepressant monotherapy** — can precipitate mania (switch to mood stabiliser first).
- **Ignoring physical health checks** — lithium requires 6–12 monthly U&E/thyroid; antipsychotics need annual metabolic screen.
- **Using benzodiazepines long-term** — tolerance and dependence; use only short-term for agitation.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - Worsening mood (severe mania or depression with suicidal thoughts)
  - Signs of lithium toxicity: vomiting, tremor, confusion, ataxia
  - Any new self-harm or suicidal behaviour
- **Follow-up:** Community mental health team (CMHT) within 1–2 weeks; lithium level / bloods as per protocol (e.g. weekly for 2 weeks then monthly).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (psychiatry/medical):** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <14, new confusion (rule out delirium), severe agitation not manageable with PRN.
- **Consider ICU:** Lithium level > 2.0 mmol/L with symptoms + renal impairment; catatonia (requires ECT); severe neuroleptic malignant syndrome; suicide attempt requiring medical admission.
- **Crash team:** Cardiac arrest, severe lithium toxicity causing arrhythmia (e.g. TdP).

rawMarkdown:
## Bipolar Affective Disorder
**One-liner:** Cyclical mood disorder with episodes of mania/hypomania and depression; high suicide risk; requires long-term mood stabilisation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Delirium (organic cause e.g. infection, metabolic, stroke):** Marked by fluctuating consciousness, disorientation, visual hallucinations — never assume “mania” without ruling out organic brain syndrome.
- **Lithium toxicity (if on treatment):** Ataxia, coarse tremor, confusion, vomiting; urgent level needed — delays can be fatal.

### Presentation
- **Mania:** Elevated/irritable mood, pressured speech, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, reckless behaviour (spending, sexual disinhibition), psychosis (delusions congruent with mood).
- **Hypomania:** Same but less severe, no psychosis, not causing major functional impairment.
- **Depression:** Low mood, anhedonia, psychomotor retardation/agitation, poor concentration, suicidal ideation.
- **Mixed episode:** Both manic and depressive symptoms simultaneously — highest suicide risk.
- **Rapid cycling:** ≥4 episodes per year.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Organic mania (hyperthyroidism, Cushing’s, medication-induced):** Check TFT, urine toxicology, cortisol if atypical features.
- **Unipolar mania (rare):** No major depressive episodes — often indistinguishable initially; history of depressive episodes is key.
- **Borderline personality disorder:** Affective instability with emptiness, identity disturbance, chronic suicidality — lack of clear episodes and absence of decreased need for sleep helps differentiate.
- **ADHD (in younger adults):** Childhood-onset, sustained inattention/hyperactivity, no discrete episodes; mania is episodic with cyclic mood.
- **Substance intoxication (cocaine, amphetamines, steroids):** Urine toxicology positive; onset linked to use.
- **Schizoaffective disorder:** Psychotic symptoms occur outside mood episodes (≥2 weeks without prominent mood symptoms).
- **Splitter:** Decreased need for sleep (not just inability to sleep) is highly specific for mania vs. affective instability in BPD.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** ECG (QTc before antipsychotics); vital signs (including temperature for NMS); urine dip for pregnancy (all women of childbearing age); urine toxicology.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, TFT (thyrotoxicosis mimics mania; lithium affects thyroid), B12/folate (if cognitive deficit), syphilis/HIV serology (if atypical), pregnancy test (if female). **Lithium level** (if known treatment) — toxic or subtherapeutic.
- **Imaging:** CT head (if first episode after 40, focal neurology, or atypical features); CT/MRI if confusion persists.
- **Special tests:** Neuropsychological testing if cognitive decline.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** DSM-5 — **Mania:** elevated/irritable mood + ≥3 of: grandiosity, decreased sleep, pressured speech, flight of ideas, distractibility, increased goal-directed activity, risky behaviour; lasting ≥1 week, causing functional impairment. **Hypomania:** same but ≥4 days, no significant impairment, no psychosis.
- **Never-miss test:** **Thyroid function** — hyperthyroidism can cause mania; untreated can worsen lithium safety. Also: **pregnancy test** before starting valproate or lithium (teratogenic).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess agitation/risk: consider benzodiazepine (lorazepam 1–2 mg IM/PO) +/- antipsychotic (haloperidol 5–10 mg IM, olanzapine 10 mg IM) — monitor QTc.
  - Safety: if imminent risk to self/others, consider urgent referral under **Mental Health Act** for detention.
  - If lithium toxicity suspected: stop lithium, get urgent level, consider IV fluids, discuss with renal/poisons.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Start **mood stabiliser** (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine for depressive phase) under specialist guidance.
  - For severe mania: lithium plus antipsychotic (e.g. olanzapine 10–20 mg od) or valproate.
  - For bipolar depression: lamotrigine or quetiapine (start low, up-titrate).
  - Monitor side effects: lithium levels (weekly until stable), renal function, thyroid, weight, metabolic profile.
  - Consider **1:1 observations** if high suicide/self-harm risk.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Maintenance: continue mood stabiliser (lithium first-line for prevention) — target level 0.6–1.0 mmol/L.
  - Psychoeducation, lifestyle (sleep hygiene, avoiding stimulants), relapse prevention plan.
  - Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) if refractory or mild symptoms.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1L 0.9% sodium chloride over 8 hours if dehydrated (common in mania), then as oral.
  - VTE prophylaxis: if prolonged immobility (e.g. catatonia) — enoxaparin 40 mg SC od.
  - Diet: normally as tolerated, high-calorie if underweight in mania.
  - Observations: if risk >5 on NEWS2, hourly; otherwise 4-hourly; dedicated mental health checks at least every shift.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Lithium (carbonate/acetate):** Start [check local policy – typical 300–400 mg od/bd], titrate to serum level 0.6–1.0 mmol/L (acute mania up to 1.2). Duration: indefinite for maintenance.
  - **Valproate (sodium valproate):** Start [check local policy – 500–1000 mg daily in divided doses], adjust to plasma level 50–100 mg/L. Duration: acute and maintenance.
  - **Lamotrigine (for depression):** Start 25 mg od, slow up-titration (weekly increase) to 200 mg od; risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
  - **Olanzapine (acute mania):** 10–20 mg PO/IM od (max 20 mg). QTc monitoring required.
  - **Quetiapine (mania or depression):** 300–600 mg/day (for mania); 150–300 mg/day for depression.
- **Common alternatives:** Carbamazepine (second-line), haloperidol (agitation), aripiprazole.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Lithium: avoid with NSAIDs, ACEi, thiazides; contraindicated in severe renal impairment.
  - Valproate: contraindicated in pregnancy (teratogenic), hepatic disease.
  - Lamotrigine: interaction with valproate (increases level, slower titration needed).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Lithium (renally cleared): reduce dose if eGFR <30; check levels frequently.
  - Valproate (hepatic metabolism): caution in liver disease.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Lithium — starting at full dose without checking baseline renal/thyroid or monitoring levels** → risk of toxicity/neurological harm. **Lamotrigine — rapid dose escalation causing Stevens-Johnson syndrome** (must follow titration protocol).

### Complications
- **Acute:** Lithium toxicity, neuroleptic malignant syndrome (antipsychotics), suicide attempt, violent behaviour.
- **Chronic:** Cognitive decline (especially with chronic lithium or repeated episodes), metabolic syndrome (weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidaemia from antipsychotics), osteoporosis (with long-term lithium), renal impairment (lithium – tubular damage), non-adherence.

### Prognosis
- Life-long illness; ~50% relapse within 1 year if medication stopped. With optimal maintenance, episodes can be reduced by 60–70%. Suicide risk ~15% lifetime (especially in mixed/rapid cycling). Functional outcome variable; early treatment and good adherence improve outcome.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check thyroid and renal function before starting lithium** — cannot monitor without baseline.
- **Document capacity to consent every visit** — mania can impair decision-making; if declining treatment, consider MHA.
- **Check QT interval on ECG before giving antipsychotics** — especially if combined with other QT-prolonging drugs.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Prescribing antidepressant monotherapy** — can precipitate mania (switch to mood stabiliser first).
- **Ignoring physical health checks** — lithium requires 6–12 monthly U&E/thyroid; antipsychotics need annual metabolic screen.
- **Using benzodiazepines long-term** — tolerance and dependence; use only short-term for agitation.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - Worsening mood (severe mania or depression with suicidal thoughts)
  - Signs of lithium toxicity: vomiting, tremor, confusion, ataxia
  - Any new self-harm or suicidal behaviour
- **Follow-up:** Community mental health team (CMHT) within 1–2 weeks; lithium level / bloods as per protocol (e.g. weekly for 2 weeks then monthly).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (psychiatry/medical):** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <14, new confusion (rule out delirium), severe agitation not manageable with PRN.
- **Consider ICU:** Lithium level > 2.0 mmol/L with symptoms + renal impairment; catatonia (requires ECT); severe neuroleptic malignant syndrome; suicide attempt requiring medical admission.
- **Crash team:** Cardiac arrest, severe lithium toxicity causing arrhythmia (e.g. TdP).
